---
title: "AI Tools for Data Science and Statistics"
subtitle: "Session 2: Context, Agent Differences, and Modeling Workflows"
author: "Erik Westlund, PhD"
date: "June 16, 2026"
bibliography: temp_source/references.bib
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    embed-resources: true
    incremental: true
    css: temp_source/slides.css
---

## Goals For Session 2

- Use context deliberately instead of relying on one-off prompts
- Compare the same task with and without a local context file
- Review how different agent/harness/model combinations can produce different analysis quality
- Practice a modeling workflow with explicit checks and interpretation limits

## Run Of Show

- Arrival and recap: 10 minutes
- Context management: 25 minutes
- Context A/B module demo: 20 minutes
- Break: 7 minutes
- Agent differences case study: 23 minutes
- Modeling practice: 35 minutes
- Wrap-up: 10 minutes

## {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Context Management
:::

::: {style="color: #ecf0f1; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center;"}
What the agent can see determines what it can follow
:::

## What Counts As Context?

- The message you type
- Files in the working directory
- Open files in the editor
- Repository-level instructions such as `AGENTS.md` or `CLAUDE.md`
- Skills, checklists, rubrics, and examples
- Prior conversation state
- Tool outputs from searches, tests, renders, or code execution

## Context Is A Design Problem

- Good context makes the desired workflow easier to follow.
- Missing context pushes the model toward generic defaults.
- Excess context can distract the model or bury the important constraint.
- Conflicting context can produce inconsistent behavior.
- Sensitive context can create privacy risk.

## What Belongs In Stable Context?

- Project structure
- Data locations and data restrictions
- Coding style and package preferences
- Output paths
- Verification steps
- Interpretation rules
- Language style rules

## What Belongs In The Prompt?

- The current task
- The specific file or output to change
- The level of detail wanted now
- Any temporary constraint for this run
- The exact question to answer before coding

## Context Files

- `AGENTS.md`: common convention for coding agents
- `CLAUDE.md`: tool-specific convention used by Claude-family workflows
- `skills/`: reusable task guidance that can be referenced from prompts
- Task prompts: short instructions for one activity or assignment

## Useful Context Pattern

```text
Use these files:
- Dataset: data/simulated/...
- Codebook: data/codebooks/...
- Skill: skills/...

First inspect the files. Then tell me what you plan to do.
Do not write the full analysis until I approve the plan.
```

## Common Context Failures

- The agent ignores the codebook.
- The agent guesses variable meanings.
- The agent writes outputs to the wrong folder.
- The agent uses a style that does not match the project.
- The agent makes causal claims from descriptive data.
- The agent produces polished interpretation before checks are complete.

## Context A/B Demonstration

Module 02 uses the same visualization task twice:

- `modules/02_context-management/without-context/`
- `modules/02_context-management/with-context/`

The second folder includes `AGENTS.md` with explicit plotting and interpretation rules.

## What To Watch For

- Does the agent find and follow `AGENTS.md`?
- Does it use the requested theme, legend position, and color scale?
- Does it label axes with units?
- Does it include source information?
- Does it avoid causal language?
- Does it explain which context rules it followed?

## {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Agent Differences
:::

::: {style="color: #ecf0f1; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center;"}
The harness and prompt are part of the analysis system
:::

## The Comparison Question

If we give similar analysis tasks to different tools, do we get equivalent work?

Important dimensions:

- Prompt detail
- Model capability
- Harness access to files
- Package choices
- Ability to run and repair code
- Quality of statistical judgment
- Quality of written interpretation

## What The Worked Experiment Shows

- More detailed prompts often produce better structure.
- Agent workflows can inspect more files and revise code.
- Chat workflows can still produce useful code when given strong context.
- Polished output can still contain errors, unsupported claims, or stale assumptions.
- Some failures are ordinary programming failures, not deep AI failures.

## What We Evaluate

- Did the code run?
- Did the analysis use the intended dataset?
- Did the model match the outcome and data structure?
- Were diagnostics used before interpretation?
- Were missingness and data quality checked?
- Were claims limited to what the data support?
- Was the work reproducible?

## Case Study Module

Module 03 is a cleaned-up case study from a worked agent-comparison experiment.

- Simple prompt versus detailed prompt
- Chat versus agent workflows
- Generated code quality
- Modeling choices
- Interpretation discipline
- Review checklist

## Main Lesson

AI output quality is not a property of the model alone.

It is produced by the full workflow:

- model
- harness
- prompt
- context
- files
- permissions
- verification loop
- analyst review

## {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Modeling Practice
:::

::: {style="color: #ecf0f1; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center;"}
Use AI to help build the workflow, then audit the workflow
:::

## Practice Task

Use `practice/tasks/02_modeling-workflow/`.

Goal:

- Build a comorbidity count from simulated maternal-health indicators.
- Fit and compare plausible count models.
- Report diagnostics and limitations.
- Save work in `practice/work/`.

## Modeling Guardrails

- Define the outcome explicitly.
- Check the outcome distribution before choosing a model.
- Compare at least two reasonable model specifications.
- Treat synthetic data as synthetic.
- Avoid causal claims.
- Keep interpretation tied to fitted model output.

## Prompting Pattern For Modeling

1. Ask for a plan before code.
2. Ask for code in small steps.
3. Run the code.
4. Paste errors back if needed.
5. Ask for a review of the model choice and interpretation.
6. Check the final result yourself.

## Wrap-Up

- Context belongs in files when it should persist.
- Prompts should reference the context that matters now.
- Agent comparisons should be judged by correctness and reproducibility, not polish.
- Modeling workflows need diagnostics and restrained interpretation.
